macro_segments.title: Introduction and the Mishnah on Nedarim Before and During Shemitah

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks and clarification of daf number
The Mishnah: One who is forbidden benefit from another before shemitah—cannot enter the field or eat overhanging fruit
The Mishnah: During shemitah—cannot enter field but can eat overhanging fruit (two interpretations)
Detailed explanation of the Mishnah's two possible readings regarding vow on shemitah versus vow before shemitah

macro_segments.title: The Amoraic Debate: Rav and Shmuel versus Rabbi Yochanan and Reish Lakish on Vow Persistence After Loss of Ownership

micro_segments.title: Rav and Shmuel: A vow made before shemitah remains binding even after shemitah arrives (property continues forbidden)
Rabbi Yochanan and Reish Lakish: A vow made before shemitah becomes permissible once shemitah arrives (when ownership ceases)
Discussion of whether forbidding access to property on shemitah is effective and how it relates to poor people's rights
The conceptual foundation of the debate: Does a person's power to forbid extend beyond their ownership?

macro_segments.title: The Gemara's Resolution: Distinguishing Between 'This Property' and 'My Property'

micro_segments.title: The Gemara proposes that the fundamental debate is whether one can forbid something even after losing ownership
A Mishnah is cited showing that a vow using the possessive 'your' is limited to the owner's lifetime, but 'this' (generic) extends beyond
The Gemara concludes there is no fundamental debate: the issue is linguistic—'my property' (possessive) stops when you lose ownership, 'this property' (generic) persists
Discussion of the Ran's interpretation and the Yerushalmi ruling on what happens when both possessive and demonstrative are used together

macro_segments.title: Shemitah Rights, Public Access, and Rabbinic Safeguards

micro_segments.title: The question of why during shemitah the vow prevents entry to the field if the poor have inherent rights to access and gather fruit
Ulla's answer: The poor's right to gather is limited to access necessary for gathering; if fruit is accessible from the boundary, entry is not required
Rabbi Shimon ben Elyakim: The poor cannot linger in the yard; rabbinic safeguard prevents all entry lest they stay longer than necessary

macro_segments.title: Mishnah on One Forbidden Benefit: Prohibitions on Lending, Borrowing, Buying, and Selling

micro_segments.title: The Mishnah lists prohibitions: cannot lend objects, borrow objects, lend money, borrow money, sell to, or buy from one another
The Gemara questions why the Mishnah includes both directions (lending and borrowing, selling and buying) when logically only one direction involves benefit
Rabbi Yose bar Chanina: The case assumes mutual vows from both sides (neither gets benefit from the other)
Abaye: It is a rabbinical safeguard (gezeirah) to forbid reciprocal transactions lest one transgress by coming to do the opposite
Discussion of benefit in sales transactions and parallel application of this principle in Avoda Zara regarding non-Jews before their holidays

topical_tags.term: Rav and Shmuel (amoraic pair on vow persistence)
Rabbi Yochanan and Reish Lakish (amoraic pair on vow limitation)
Rabbi Shimon ben Elyakim (on lingering in field)
Rabbi Yose bar Chanina (mutual vow interpretation)
Access to field/yard (eino yored letoch sadehu)
